title: GPS or the map in your head
summary: A young Istanbul taxi driver and his uncle, also a driver for thirty years, argue at a tea stand between shifts about whether the GPS app is helping or quietly ruining the trade.

body.speaker: Uncle Hasan
body.text: You are late again, Murat. Park behind mine. I have ordered tea. Sit down. I want to talk before your night shift, because last week three passengers told me you were following the phone.

body.speaker: Murat
body.text: Three passengers complained? Or three mentioned it?

body.speaker: Uncle Hasan
body.text: Two complained. One mentioned it kindly. But all three noticed, which is the part that matters. A driver who is looking at his phone is not, in the passenger's eyes, a driver. He is a delivery boy with a car.

body.speaker: Murat
body.text: Amca, with respect — I am twenty-six. I have been driving the taxi for two years. You have been driving for thirty. You know every street in Beyoğlu from memory. I do not. The app gets me there faster while I am still learning.

body.speaker: Uncle Hasan
body.text: Does it? Last Tuesday a fare asked you to go from Taksim to Karaköy at six. The app sent you down Sıraselviler. Sıraselviler at six is a car park. A driver with a map in his head would have cut through Cihangir, lost three minutes on the descent, and saved fifteen on the arrival.

body.speaker: Murat
body.text: How do you know that was mine?

body.speaker: Uncle Hasan
body.text: The passenger was a friend of your aunt. She called me, embarrassed. I am telling you because I would rather you hear it from me than read it as a one-star rating next month.

body.speaker: Murat
body.text: Fair. But here is the other side. Last month I picked up a tourist from the airport who wanted Kadıköy. Without the app I would have guessed an old route through traffic. The app saw an accident on the bridge and rerouted me. We saved forty minutes. He tipped me well. The app does see things I cannot see.

body.speaker: Uncle Hasan
body.text: Yes. I will not pretend the app is useless. Traffic, accidents, closures — it sees them, and I do not. The fight is not 'app versus no app'. The fight is who is driving. If the app is driving and you are obeying, you are a passenger with a steering wheel. If you are driving and the app is whispering, then you are a driver.

body.speaker: Murat
body.text: And how do I know the difference? Practically?

body.speaker: Uncle Hasan
body.text: Try this for one month. The first ten minutes of every shift, drive without the phone. Anywhere. Just drive. Get lost if you get lost. After one month, you will start to feel the city again. Then turn the app on, but with the sound off. Glance at it, do not listen. The day you can argue with the app — say 'no, this way is better at this hour' and be right half the time — you have become a driver.

body.speaker: Murat
body.text: And the other half of the time?

body.speaker: Uncle Hasan
body.text: The other half you will be wrong, and the passenger will pay an extra twenty lira, and you will remember it forever. That is how the map gets into your head. Not from a class. From being wrong, in front of a paying passenger, in a city you thought you knew. Drink your tea before it gets cold.

annotations.meaningVi: đi theo điện thoại (làm theo GPS một cách máy móc)
'chú / bác' trong tiếng Thổ Nhĩ Kỳ
từ Taksim đến Karaköy (hai khu Istanbul)
một bãi đỗ xe (ý: tắc cứng)
hành khách có vô-lăng (ý: chỉ ngồi sau tay lái mà không thực sự lái)
hai mươi lira (đồng tiền Thổ Nhĩ Kỳ)

annotations.phrase: in a city you thought you knew
annotations.meaningVi: trong một thành phố mà mình tưởng đã thuộc

grammarNotes.title: Conditionals + present perfect for experience
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 để nói về quá khứ giả định: 'A driver with a map in his head would have cut through Cihangir, lost three minutes on the descent, and saved fifteen on the arrival.' / 'Without the app I would have guessed an old route through traffic.' (would have + V3). Bài cũng dùng hiện tại hoàn thành chỉ kinh nghiệm và khoảng thời gian: 'I have been driving the taxi for two years.' / 'You have been driving for thirty.' — kết hợp với 'for + thời lượng' để cho thấy hành động bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn tiếp diễn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses the 3rd conditional for hypothetical past: 'A driver with a map in his head would have cut through Cihangir, lost three minutes on the descent, and saved fifteen on the arrival.' / 'Without the app I would have guessed an old route through traffic.' (would have + V3). The text also uses the present perfect for experience and duration: 'I have been driving the taxi for two years.' / 'You have been driving for thirty.' — combined with 'for + duration' to show an action that started in the past and continues now.

translationVi:
Uncle Hasan: Lại trễ rồi Murat. Đậu sau xe chú. Chú đã gọi trà cho cả hai. Ngồi đi. Chú muốn nói với cháu trước ca đêm, vì tuần trước ba hành khách bảo chú là cháu đang đi theo điện thoại.

Murat: Ba người phàn nàn? Hay ba người nhắc tới?

Uncle Hasan: Hai phàn nàn. Một nhắc tử tế. Nhưng cả ba đều để ý, đó mới là điều quan trọng. Một tài xế đang nhìn điện thoại, trong mắt khách, không phải tài xế. Là cậu giao hàng có ô tô.

Murat: Amca, với tất cả sự tôn trọng — cháu hai sáu. Cháu chạy taxi hai năm. Chú chạy ba mươi. Chú thuộc lòng từng phố ở Beyoğlu. Cháu thì không. App giúp cháu đến nơi nhanh hơn trong khi cháu còn học.

Uncle Hasan: Thật thế à? Thứ Ba tuần trước một khách bảo cháu chạy từ Taksim đến Karaköy lúc sáu tối. App đưa cháu xuống Sıraselviler. Sıraselviler lúc sáu là bãi đỗ xe. Một tài xế có bản đồ trong đầu sẽ cắt qua Cihangir, mất ba phút đoạn dốc, và tiết kiệm mười lăm phút khi đến nơi.

Murat: Sao chú biết đó là khách cháu?

Uncle Hasan: Vì khách đó là bạn của thím cháu. Cô ấy gọi chú, ngại. Cô ấy không muốn cháu biết. Chú nói cho cháu vì chú thà cháu nghe từ chú còn hơn đọc nó dưới dạng đánh giá một sao tháng tới.

Murat: Đúng. Đúng vậy. Nhưng có mặt khác. Tháng trước cháu đón một du khách từ sân bay muốn đi Kadıköy. Không có app cháu sẽ đoán một tuyến cũ qua chỗ tắc. App thấy một vụ tai nạn trên cầu và đổi tuyến. Tiết kiệm bốn mươi phút. Anh ta tip cháu khá. App quả thật thấy những thứ cháu không thấy.

Uncle Hasan: Đúng. Chú sẽ không vờ là app vô dụng. Tắc, tai nạn, đóng đường — nó thấy, chú không thấy. Cuộc tranh luận không phải 'có app hay không'. Cuộc tranh luận là ai đang lái. Nếu app đang lái và cháu chỉ tuân theo, cháu là hành khách có vô-lăng. Nếu cháu đang lái và app chỉ thì thầm, thì cháu là tài xế.

Murat: Và cháu phân biệt thế nào? Một cách thực tế?

Uncle Hasan: Thử cách này một tháng. Mười phút đầu mỗi ca, lái không điện thoại. Đi đâu cũng được. Cứ lái. Lạc thì lạc. Sau một tháng, cháu sẽ bắt đầu cảm lại thành phố. Sau đó bật app lên, nhưng tắt tiếng. Liếc nó, đừng nghe nó. Ngày cháu có thể cãi với app — nói 'không, giờ này đi đường này hơn' và đúng một nửa số lần — cháu đã thành tài xế.

Murat: Còn nửa số lần kia?

Uncle Hasan: Nửa kia cháu sẽ sai, và khách sẽ trả thêm hai mươi lira, và cháu sẽ nhớ mãi. Đó là cách bản đồ vào đầu cháu. Không phải từ lớp học. Từ việc sai, trước mặt khách trả tiền, trong một thành phố cháu tưởng đã thuộc. Uống trà đi kẻo nguội.

questions.prompt: Why has Hasan called Murat to the tea stand?
options: Because three passengers last week said Murat was following the phone — two complained, one mentioned it kindly

questions.prompt: What does Hasan say a phone-watching driver looks like to passengers?
options: A delivery boy with a car, not a driver

questions.prompt: What is Murat's defence about his age and experience?
options: He is twenty-six and has driven the taxi for two years; Hasan has driven for thirty and knows Beyoğlu from memory — the app helps him learn
The app sent Murat down Sıraselviler at six p.m., when it is a car park; cutting through Cihangir would have saved fifteen minutes

questions.prompt: How does Hasan know it was Murat's fare?
options: The passenger was a friend of Murat's aunt and called Hasan, embarrassed

questions.prompt: What example does Murat give in defence of the app?
options: A tourist from the airport to Kadıköy — the app saw an accident on the bridge and rerouted; they saved forty minutes
A short trip in heavy rain

questions.prompt: How does Hasan reframe the real disagreement?
options: Not 'app versus no app' but 'who is driving' — if the app drives and you obey, you are a passenger with a steering wheel; if you drive and the app whispers, you are a driver

questions.prompt: What practical test does Hasan suggest for the first month?
options: First ten minutes of every shift, drive without the phone — anywhere, even if lost
Pay a senior driver to teach him

questions.prompt: After that month, how should Murat use the app?
options: Turn it on but with the sound off; glance, do not listen

questions.prompt: When is Murat 'a driver', by Hasan's test?
options: When he can argue with the app — say a route is better at that hour — and be right half the time
When he stops using the app
When he gets a five-star rating
When he buys a new car

questions.prompt: What does Hasan say about being wrong the other half of the time?
options: The passenger pays an extra twenty lira and Murat remembers forever — that is how the map gets into his head
He should hide it from his uncle

The right question is not whether to use GPS but who is actually driving — a real driver uses the app as advice, keeps building a map in his head, and learns most from being wrong in front of paying passengers.
GPS apps should be banned for taxis.
Young drivers should not be trusted.
questions.explanation: Whole dialogue, especially turn 9 and the last turn.

Murat: Three passengers complained? Or three mentioned it?
Uncle Hasan: Two complained. One mentioned it kindly. But all three noticed, which is the part that matters. A driver who is looking at his phone is not, in the passenger's eyes, a driver. He is a delivery {{b2}} with a car.

Uncle Hasan: Does it? Last Tuesday a fare asked you to go from Taksim to Karaköy at six. The app sent you down Sıraselviler. Sıraselviler at six is a car {{b4}}. A driver with a map in his head would have cut through Cihangir, lost three minutes on the descent, and saved fifteen on the arrival.
Murat: How do you know that was mine?
Uncle Hasan: The passenger was a friend of your aunt. She called me, embarrassed. I am telling you because I would rather you hear it from me than read it as a one-{{b5}} rating next month.
Murat: Fair. But here is the other side. Last month I picked up a tourist from the airport who wanted Kadıköy. Without the app I would have guessed an old route through traffic. The app saw an accident on the bridge and {{b6}} me. We saved forty minutes. He tipped me well. The app does see things I cannot see.
Uncle Hasan: Yes. I will not pretend the app is useless. Traffic, accidents, closures — it sees them, and I do not. The fight is not 'app versus no app'. The fight is who is {{b7}}. If the app is driving and you are obeying, you are a passenger with a steering wheel. If you are driving and the app is whispering, then you are a driver.
Murat: And how do I know the difference? Practically?
Uncle Hasan: Try this for one month. The first ten minutes of every shift, drive without the phone. Anywhere. Just drive. Get lost if you get lost. After one month, you will start to feel the city again. Then turn the app on, but with the {{b8}} off. Glance at it, do not listen. The day you can {{b9}} with the app — say 'no, this way is better at this hour' and be right half the time — you have become a driver.
Murat: And the other half of the time?
Uncle Hasan: The other half you will be {{b10}}, and the passenger will pay an extra twenty lira, and you will remember it forever. That is how the map gets into your head. Not from a class. From being wrong, in front of a paying passenger, in a city you thought you {{b11}}. Drink your tea before it gets cold.

blanks.explanation: A delivery boy with a car.
A city you thought you knew.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Hasan says you only become a driver by being wrong in front of paying passengers. Where in your own life have you learned a skill that no class could teach you — only the cost of being publicly wrong?
